core_misconceptions: Outliers are just mistakes that should always be removed.
A single outlier does not have an impact if the sample size is large enough.
Including an outlier is always more honest than excluding it.
object.body_markdown: When you examine a set of data, you are often looking for the narrative of the group. However, sometimes a single data point sits so far outside the expected range that it changes the entire picture. These points are called outliers, and they function like the loud voice in a crowded room—they can provide vital information, or they can entirely drown out the conversation of everyone else. Understanding how to handle them is a core skill for any staff member reviewing reports on performance or student outcomes. ## The three ways to see them - Inclusion: Keeping the outlier in the data set to acknowledge the full reality of an event, even if it is rare. - Exclusion: Removing the outlier to see a more representative picture of the core group's typical experience. - Segmentation: Reporting the main group and the outlier as two separate stories, rather than blending them into one confusing number. Outliers are not inherently bad data. A student finishing a doctoral program in three years instead of six is an outlier. A donor making a gift ten times larger than the typical donation is an outlier. The choice to include, exclude, or segment depends on whether you want to describe the 'usual' or capture the 'exceptional.' ## Why this matters for Harvard staff In your daily work, you are often tasked with interpreting data to inform policy or resource allocation. If you see an outlier in a dataset, you have to decide if that person or event represents a flaw in the system, an success story to be replicated, or a piece of noise that shouldn't impact broad conclusions. If a report on office response times includes one request that took three months due to a unique legal complication, keeping that number in the mean will suggest the entire department is slow. If you don't account for outliers, you risk misinterpreting institutional trends or misallocating support based on a set of data that is being pulled in an unrepresentative direction. ## A reading example Imagine you are reviewing a report on the 'Time to Completion' for a cohort of 50 certificate students. The report states, 'Our typical student finishes in 12 months.' However, you notice the data ranges from 9 months to 36 months, with the latter figure belonging to a single student who paused their studies for a medical leave. If the report includes that 36-month student in the mean, it paints an inaccurate picture of the program's intensity for the other 49 students. Before trusting the finding, consider if the report has segmented the 'medical leave' cases from the 'standard completion' timeline. ## What to practice The next time you see a summary report that seems at odds with your professional experience, use the Claim-Support-Question routine to interrogate the presence of outliers: 1. Claim: What is the main finding being asserted by this summary figure? 2. Support: What evidence do I have that an outlier might be inflating or deflating this result? 3. Question: If we reported this data without the extreme values, how would the conclusion change, and which version is more helpful for our staff goals?
